---
title: "Rotational feedback (planned)"
---

::: {.callout-important title="Status: not yet implemented"}
Rotational feedback is rung 5 of the [validation ladder](../benchmarks/index.qmd)
and is not yet in the code. This page records the intended physics and the
design constraints so the implementation is shaped correctly from the start.
:::

A redistribution of surface mass (ice, ocean water) and the resulting solid-Earth
deformation change the planet's inertia tensor, which perturbs the rotation
vector — **true polar wander** and length-of-day changes. The shifted rotation
in turn alters the centrifugal potential, which is itself a **degree-2** surface
load that drives further deformation and sea-level change. This two-way coupling
is the *rotational feedback*.

## Intended formulation

FastEarth3D will follow the **time-domain** rotational theory of
@MartinecHagedoorn2014, which is consistent with the explicit memory-stress
machinery already in place: the centrifugal potential perturbation is assembled
as an extra degree-2 forcing on the same per-degree operator, and the Liouville
equation for the rotation vector is integrated alongside the Maxwell memory.
This avoids the linearized, normal-mode rotational treatments that do not
compose with a time-domain, 3D-viscosity solver.

## Design constraints to honour

- **Degree-2, tidal Love numbers.** The rotational deformation uses *tidal*
  (not loading) Love numbers; the centrifugal forcing is purely degree 2.
- **Fix the fluid Love number $k_f$ from the observed flattening**
  [@Mitrovica2005]. Computing $k_f$ from the assumed lithosphere thickness leads
  to the well-known "lithosphere-thickness paradox" in rotational stability;
  pinning $k_f$ to the observed hydrostatic flattening avoids it.
- **Couple through the SLE.** The rotational potential is a load like any other,
  so it enters the [sea-level equation](sea-level.qmd) self-consistently rather
  than as a bolt-on correction.

## Validation target

Spada et al. (2011) test 3/2 (polar motion) is the planned benchmark
[@Spada2011], cross-checked against the rotational cases of the GIA
intercomparison.
